title: workmanager (Flutter)
objectives: Wire Android WorkManager and iOS BGTaskScheduler
Handle constraints, retries and platform limits

sections.markdown: The Flutter `workmanager` package wraps Android WorkManager and iOS BGTaskScheduler so you can run Dart work in the background — even after app kill (Android) — with constraints and retries.

iOS is strict: BGTasks are opportunistic and rare. Never assume iOS will run your sync on time. Use it for eventually-consistent work (upload logs, refresh caches), not exact schedules. Android WorkManager is much more reliable.
Doing UI work or Firebase inits from the background isolate without re-initializing plugins. Background runs in a fresh isolate — you must set up plugins/DI inside `callbackDispatcher`.
For pure Android use cases, plain WorkManager via native code + platform channel is more mature. This plugin is best when you need cross-platform simplicity and iOS 'good-enough' background work.

flashcards.back: Android WorkManager + iOS BGTaskScheduler behind one Dart API.
Opportunistic, rare, no guaranteed timing. Never rely for exact schedules.
Fresh isolate — plugins/DI must be re-initialized inside callbackDispatcher.

quiz.explain: iOS decides when to run them.
It runs in a fresh isolate.

challenges.text: Run a one-off task that logs from background.
Periodic task with a network constraint that syncs data.
Wire retries with backoff for transient failures.
Design a hybrid: FCM data message triggers WorkManager for guaranteed processing.

interviewQuestions: Android WorkManager vs iOS BGTaskScheduler expectations?
How do you constrain and retry?
